cure.note: EXCEPTION to the p-* group: pastirma is NOT brine-cured and NOT smoked — it is DRY-salt cured then AIR-DRIED raw and eaten uncooked. Marianski dry cure = 25 g salt + 5 g Cure #2 per kg (~0.5%), ~1 week fridge, wash, press, then çemen (fenugreek/garlic/paprika/cumin) paste coat and hang. Because it is a long air-dried RAW product, the correct curing salt is Cure #2 (nitrite + nitrate reservoir), NOT Cure #1. Traditional versions use salt only, but nitrite/nitrate is the safety-recommended option.

smoke.note: No smoke. Air-dry in ventilated area 10-15°C, ~60% RH for ~5 days until çemen paste is dry, then further dry as desired. Cold room only — must dry, not cook.

safe.note: Raw dry-cured product — no thermal pasteurization floor. Safety comes from salt + Cure #2 (nitrite/nitrate) + drying to reduced water activity, not from cooking.

process.note: Dry-salt cure 2-7 days, rinse/soak, press to expel moisture, coat in çemen paste, air-dry, slice paper-thin against grain. Eaten raw.

flag: MISCLASSIFIED: pastirma is a RAW dry-cured/air-dried product (not brined, not cooked/smoked) — if a curing salt is used it must be Cure #2, not Cure #1; requires proper drying (reduced aw) and cold storage.
rationale: Odd one out of the p-* group: dry-cured raw air-dried beef with çemen, no brine and no cook — kept calc but flagged Cure #2 + drying safety.

cure.note: Brine-cured (salt/cure in the wet brine, so dry-calc salt=0/cure=None is correct). Standard corned-beef/pastrami brine per liter water: 90 g salt + 18 g Cure #1 (Prague Powder #1, 6.25% sodium nitrite) + 10 g sugar + coriander/pepper/garlic; cure ~4 days per kg submerged at 2-4°C. Cure #1 (not #2) is correct — this is a cooked short-process product. Deckel (point/deckle) is fatty — brine to core.

smoke.note: Rinse, coat with cracked pepper+coriander rub, smoke ~107°C (225°F) to ~71°C internal, then wrap and take to ~95°C (203°F) for slice-tender. Steaming to finish is the classic tender route. Pink color is from the cure, not undercooking.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle beef 63°C)
safe.note: Displayed conservative floor 63°C/145°F. A firmer deli slice can be pulled ~63°C; slice-tender pastrami is taken to ~95-96°C/203-205°F to render collagen. Brine-cured with nitrite + cooked.

process.note: Brine → rinse → pepper/coriander rub → smoke → wrap/steam to tender. Point/deckle is the fatty muscle.

rationale: Confirmed brine-cured with Cure #1; documented 63°C deli floor vs ~95°C slice-tender target; calc 0/None correct for a brined item.

cure.note: Same brine cure as beef pastrami: per liter 90 g salt + 18 g Cure #1 + 10 g sugar, ~4 days/kg submerged at 2-4°C. Cure #1 correct (cooked short-process). Salt/cure live in the brine → dry-calc 0/None is expected.

smoke.ref: AmazingRibs — Homemade Pastrami (method applied to lamb)
smoke.note: Pepper/coriander crust, smoke ~107°C (225°F) to ~71°C then wrap to ~93-95°C for tender slices (lamb breast/shoulder).

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle lamb 63°C)
safe.note: Whole-muscle lamb floor 63°C/145°F + rest; tender-slice target ~93-95°C. Nitrite-cured + cooked.

process.note: Brine-cure → rub → smoke → wrap/steam to tender.

rationale: Brine-cured Cure #1 confirmed; lamb whole-muscle floor 63°C documented; calc 0/None correct.

cure.ref: GlebeKitchen / meatsandsausages — Montreal Smoked Meat brine
cure.note: Wet/dry cure using Prague Powder #1 (Cure #1, 6.25% sodium nitrite) — nitrite develops the cured pink color and inhibits C. botulinum; cure the brisket ~8 days at 2-4°C, turning daily. Cure #1 correct (cooked). Salt/cure in the cure → dry-calc 0/None expected.

smoke.note: Coriander/black-pepper crust, smoke ~120°C (225-250°F, maple) to ~71-75°C internal, THEN STEAM 2-3 h until ~95-96°C (~203°F) probe-tender — steaming is the defining Montreal step.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle beef 63°C)
safe.note: Displayed floor 63°C/145°F; authentic texture requires steaming to ~95-96°C. Nitrite-cured + cooked + steamed.

process.note: Cure ~8 days → pepper/coriander crust → smoke → STEAM to tender. Steaming distinguishes it from NY pastrami.

rationale: Confirmed Cure #1 brine/cure + smoke-then-steam to ~95°C; documented 63°C floor; calc 0/None correct.

cure.ref: AmazingRibs (Meathead) — Homemade Pastrami / Curing Meats Safely
cure.note: Classic Katz's-style wet cure = salt + sodium nitrite (Prague Powder #1 / Cure #1, 6.25% nitrite) brine, 1-4 weeks; AmazingRibs stresses use ONLY Cure #1 (not saltpeter, Tender Quick, Himalayan, or Cure #2). Cure #1 correct for this cooked product. Salt/cure in brine → dry-calc 0/None expected.

smoke.note: Pepper/coriander crust, smoke ~107°C (225°F) to ~71°C (160°F), then wrap and take to ~95°C (203°F) for slice-tender; ~6 h total. Bright pink interior is cure color, not undercooked.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle beef 63°C)
safe.note: Displayed floor 63°C/145°F (firmer deli slice); classic tender pastrami taken to ~95-96°C/203-205°F. Nitrite-cured + cooked.

process.note: Brine-cure → rinse → pepper/coriander rub → smoke to 160°F → wrap/steam to 203°F.

rationale: Confirmed brine-cured with Cure #1 (AmazingRibs explicitly warns only Cure #1); documented 63°C deli floor vs ~95°C slice-tender; calc 0/None correct.

cure.note: Brine-cured turkey breast: per liter 90 g salt + 18 g Cure #1 + 10 g sugar + pepper/coriander, cure a few days at 2-4°C. Cure #1 (nitrite) correct — cooked short-process. Salt/cure in brine → dry-calc 0/None expected.

smoke.note: Pepper/coriander crust, warm-smoke ~110-120°C to internal 74°C (165°F). Do NOT overcook lean turkey breast; pull at 74°C, no need for 95°C collagen render.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (poultry 74°C)
safe.note: POULTRY floor 74°C/165°F (salmonella) — higher than the 63°C beef-pastrami floor. Cook turkey pastrami to 74°C.

process.note: Brine-cure → rub → smoke to 74°C internal. Lean breast — cook to floor, not to tender-render.

flag: POULTRY: safety floor is 74°C/165°F, not the 63°C used for beef pastrami.
rationale: Brine-cured Cure #1 confirmed; corrected safety expectation to poultry 74°C; calc 0/None correct for a brined item.

smoke.note: Not cured/smoked: yogurt-spice marinated thigh/breast stacked and roasted on a vertical spit (or high-heat oven ~260°C/500°F then broil), shaving cooked outer layers. Marinate 2-24 h refrigerated.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (poultry 74°C)
safe.note: Poultry must reach 74°C/165°F (salmonella/campylobacter). Shaved layers are pulled only once each reaches 74°C.

process.note: Marinate → stack on spit → roast, shaving cooked exterior in layers to 74°C.

flag: POULTRY: shave only fully-cooked outer layers to 74°C.
rationale: Marinated not cured (no nitrite) — cure field omitted; poultry floor 74°C confirmed.

smoke.note: Spiced lamb (baharat/amba style) stacked and roasted on a vertical spit, shaving cooked exterior; no cure/nitrite.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle lamb/beef 63°C)
safe.note: Whole-muscle lamb/beef floor 63°C/145°F + 3 min rest; shaved surface layers are seared. Cook per doneness above the floor.

process.note: Marinate → stack on spit → roast/shave cooked layers.

rationale: Marinated whole-muscle lamb, no cure — omitted cure field; whole-muscle floor 63°C.

smoke.note: Yogurt/spice-marinated lamb+beef slices stacked on a vertical spit and roasted, shaving cooked exterior; no cure/nitrite.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle beef/lamb 63°C)
safe.note: Whole-muscle beef/lamb floor 63°C/145°F + rest; shaved layers seared as cut. If any ground meat is layered in, treat as 71°C.

process.note: Marinate → stack whole-muscle slices on spit → roast/shave.

rationale: Marinated whole-muscle lamb/beef, no cure — omitted cure field; floor 63°C (71°C if ground layers present).

smoke.note: Mixed lamb/beef (often with turkey or chicken) marinated, stacked and spit-roasted; no cure/nitrite.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart
safe.note: Safety-conservative: a mixed stack that may include poultry must reach 74°C/165°F. A beef/lamb-only mix pasteurizes at 63°C, but display the poultry-safe 74°C floor.

process.note: Marinate → stack mixed meats → roast/shave cooked layers.

flag: MIXED STACK: use 74°C if poultry is included; shave only fully-cooked layers.
rationale: Marinated, no cure — omitted cure field; chose conservative 74°C because a mixed stack may include poultry.

smoke.note: Marinated turkey thigh/breast stacked and spit-roasted (or oven 260°C then broil), shaving cooked exterior; no cure/nitrite.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (poultry 74°C)
safe.note: Poultry floor 74°C/165°F. Shave only layers that have reached 74°C.

process.note: Marinate → stack on spit → roast/shave cooked layers to 74°C.

flag: POULTRY: shave only fully-cooked outer layers to 74°C.
rationale: Marinated poultry, no cure — omitted cure field; poultry floor 74°C.

smoke.note: Smoke the point at ~121°C (250°F). When it hits ~95°C (195°F), cube ~1 inch, dust with rub + a little sugar, sauce, and return to caramelize/brown the bark; finish probe-tender ~95-100°C (203°F+).

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle beef 63°C)
safe.note: Displayed floor 63°C/145°F; burnt ends are always taken far past this (~95-100°C) to render the point's collagen/fat. No cure.

process.note: Smoke point to ~195°F → cube → rub/sugar/sauce → return to bark up and tenderize.

rationale: AmazingRibs: 250°F smoke, cube at 195°F, finish tender ~203°F; no cure — cure field omitted; display floor 63°C.

cure.note: OPTIONAL cure. AmazingRibs notes a few Texas hot-link recipes use curing salt with nitrite but MOST do not — cooked fresh and eaten. If cured, use Cure #1 (2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite) and cure overnight; Cure #1 is correct (short cooked process, not Cure #2). Cure is recommended if smoking low/slow (long time in the 4-60°C zone).

smoke.note: ~25% fat coarse beef/pork, pepper+cayenne dominant. Smoke steady ~107°C (225°F) to internal 71°C (160°F), ~1-2 h. If uncured, keep the cook moving to limit danger-zone time.

safe.note: Ground/emulsified sausage floor 71°C/160°F (AmazingRibs pulls at 160°F). Higher than whole-muscle because grinding distributes surface bacteria.

process.note: Grind (coarse) → season → stuff → smoke 225°F to 160°F internal.

flag: GROUND-MEAT floor 71°C. Cure #1 optional but recommended for low/slow smoking.
rationale: AmazingRibs: 225°F smoke to 160°F; cure optional (most omit) — noted Cure #1 if used; ground-meat floor 71°C.

smoke.ref: ThermoWorks / Meat Church — Pork Belly Burnt Ends
smoke.note: Cube belly ~2 in, rub, smoke ~121°C (250°F) ~3 h; then pan with butter/honey/BBQ sauce, cover, return until ~93-96°C (200-205°F) probe-tender; uncover to set glaze.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle pork 63°C)
safe.note: Displayed floor 63°C/145°F; belly burnt ends are cooked to ~93-96°C (200-205°F) to render fat/collagen. No cure.

process.ref: ThermoWorks — Smoked Pork Belly Burnt Ends
process.note: Cube → rub → smoke 250°F ~3 h → braise in sauce/butter/honey to 200-205°F → glaze.

rationale: 250°F smoke then braise to ~200-205°F tender; no cure — cure field omitted; display floor 63°C pork.

smoke.ref: ThermoWorks — Porchetta Temperature Roadmap / RecipeTin Eats
smoke.note: Herb-rubbed rolled pork (loin wrapped in belly). Air-dry skin 1-2 days in fridge; roast/smoke low ~135°C (275°F) to internal ~63-71°C, then high blast ~260°C (500°F) 15-20 min to crisp/crackle the skin (reverse-sear style).

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (whole-muscle pork 63°C)
safe.note: Rolled whole-muscle pork floor 63°C/145°F + 3 min rest (many take to ~71°C for sliceability). Because it is rolled (former surface becomes interior), ensure the core reaches the floor. No cure/nitrite.

process.note: Season → roll/tie belly around loin → dry skin → low roast to core temp → high blast for crackling → rest.

rationale: Rolled pork roast, no cure — cure field omitted; core to 63°C floor (rolled roast), skin crisped at high heat.

cure.ref: FDA Fish & Fishery Products Hazards & Controls Guidance, Ch.5 (parasites) / classic gravlax cure
cure.note: Salt + sugar dry cure (classic ~2:1 salt:sugar) + dill/pepper, NO nitrite — correct, gravlax uses no curing salt. Cure ~2-3 days refrigerated (<4°C), weighted. The salt/sugar cure does NOT kill parasites: FDA notes nematode larvae survive weeks in 21% brine.

smoke.note: No smoke — gravlax is cured only, eaten raw/uncooked.

safe.ref: FDA Fish & Fishery Products Guidance Ch.5 — parasite destruction by freezing
safe.note: Raw cured fish — no thermal floor. PARASITE control by freezing: −20°C for 7 days, OR −35°C until solid then held −35°C for 15 h, OR −35°C then −20°C for 24 h. Use previously-frozen (sushi/sashimi-grade) salmon; cure and store <4°C.

process.note: Freeze fish per FDA schedule → salt/sugar/dill cure weighted 2-3 days at <4°C → rinse, slice thin. No cook, no smoke.

flag: RAW FISH: mandatory parasite-killing freeze (−20°C/7 days or −35°C schedule) before curing; cure/store <4°C. Salt+sugar cure alone does NOT kill parasites.
rationale: Salt+sugar raw cure with no nitrite is correct (kept salt=40/cure=None); the real control is the FDA parasite freeze, flagged.

cure.ref: Oregon State Seafood — Smoked Fish Compendium Ch.7 / Food Safety Magazine — Hot & Cold Smoked Fish
cure.note: CURE-CRITICAL. Salt is the primary control and must reach ≥3.5% water-phase salt (WPS) in vacuum/reduced-O2 pack, or ≥2.5% WPS air-packed, to control C. botulinum type E (non-proteolytic, grows at fridge temps). NITRITE optional but recommended: FDA permits up to 200 ppm sodium nitrite in smoked salmon as an added C. botulinum barrier — if added, use Cure #1. Salt alone does not fully control Listeria (halotolerant) — cold-smoke + strict refrigeration + short shelf life are additional barriers. Current cure=None: salt is present but nitrite is NOT — recommend adding optional nitrite and verifying ≥3.5% WPS.

smoke.ref: Oregon State Seafood Compendium Ch.7 / Food Safety Magazine
smoke.note: COLD smoke: fish held no higher than ~30°C (≤33°C max) so it never cooks/coagulates — it is NOT pasteurized by the smoke. Form pellicle, then cold-smoke hours-to-days after achieving target salt.

safe.ref: Oregon State Seafood Compendium Ch.7 (C. botulinum type E / Listeria controls)
safe.note: NO thermal pasteurization (cold process). Safety = ≥3.5% WPS (vacuum) / ≥2.5% (air) + cold-smoke ≤30°C + optional nitrite ≤200 ppm + continuous refrigeration ≤3.3°C and short shelf life. Use previously-frozen fish for parasites (FDA freeze schedule).

process.note: Salt/brine to target WPS → drain/pellicle → cold-smoke ≤30°C → vacuum pack → keep ≤3.3°C. Not cooked.

flag: CURE-CRITICAL / RAW COLD-SMOKED: C. botulinum type E + Listeria hazard. MUST reach ≥3.5% water-phase salt (vacuum) or ≥2.5% (air), cold-smoke ≤30°C, refrigerate ≤3.3°C, short shelf life; optional nitrite ≤200 ppm (Cure #1) recommended. Freeze fish first for parasites. Current recipe has salt but no nitrite — recommend adding optional nitrite and confirming WPS.
rationale: Highest-risk item: kept salt=40 but flagged that nitrite is recommended and the real controls are ≥3.5% WPS, cold-smoke ≤30°C, and strict refrigeration against botulinum-E/Listeria.

cure.ref: Taste of Artisan / Marianski — Hot Smoked Mackerel brine
cure.note: Light salt brine (flavor/pellicle) ~1-2 h then pellicle-dry — NO nitrite needed because it is HOT-smoked (fully cooked), unlike cold-smoked lox. No curing salt required.

smoke.ref: Taste of Artisan — Hot Smoked Mackerel
smoke.note: HOT smoke, stepped: ~30°C/85°F light smoke 30-60 min → ~50°C/120°F 30-45 min → raise to ~80°C/175-180°F until internal 63°C (145°F); hold at temp ~30 min. This cooks the fish.

safe.ref: USDA FSIS Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart (fish 63°C)
safe.note: Hot-smoked = cooked: internal 63°C/145°F. Holding ~30 min at temp adds margin. Contrast with cold-smoked lox which is never cooked.

process.ref: Taste of Artisan — Hot Smoked Mackerel
process.note: Brine 1-2 h → pellicle-dry ~2 h → hot-smoke stepped to internal 63°C.

rationale: Hot-smoked (cooked) — only a flavor brine, no nitrite; cook floor 63°C confirmed; distinct from the cold-smoked lox path.
